title: Tell me about yourself — walk me through your background and resume.

transcript.text: Tell me about yourself — walk me through your background and resume.

blocks.md:
## "Tell me about yourself" — assuming a strong-tech-company hiring-manager round
> 💬 No company named, so I'm pitching this as a general senior-backend HM intro. If this is for Uber/Amazon/Google specifically, tell me and I'll re-tune the "why I'm here" ending.

Not STAR for this one — it's a career-arc walkthrough. Present → 2-3 highlights → why I'm excited. ~150 words spoken.

🎙️ **Say-it script:**

> So I'm a backend engineer, about three and a half years in, mostly high-throughput distributed systems. Right now I'm an SDE-3 at Walmart Data Ventures, building supplier-facing data APIs — the stuff Pepsi and Coca-Cola use to pull their sales and inventory data. Spring Boot, Java 17, Kafka, a lot of GCP.
>
> Before Walmart I was at a startup called Good Creator Co. That's where I grew up as an engineer, honestly. Five-person backend team, and I ended up sole architect on six production services. My favorite piece was moving our analytics off Postgres onto ClickHouse — we were doing 10 million writes a day and Postgres was buckling. Got query times from 30 seconds down to 12, and cut infra cost about 30%.
>
> I started at PayU on loan disbursal — first real job, real customer money moving. Learned reliability the hard way there.
>
> What I want next is platform work at bigger scale. That's the pull.

💬 **Even-shorter version:**
> Backend engineer, three and a half years, high-throughput systems. SDE-3 at Walmart now on supplier data APIs; before that I solo-architected six services at a startup — the highlight was a ClickHouse migration at 10M writes a day. Started at PayU on loan disbursal. Looking for platform work at bigger scale next.

---

**Likely follow-ups:**
- "Tell me more about the ClickHouse migration." → That's my hero story (G1) — happy to go deep: RabbitMQ-buffered sinker, batch-of-1000-or-5-sec flush, MergeTree partitioned by month, two-week dual-write for zero data loss.
- "Why are you leaving Walmart?" → Positive framing only: I want platform/infra impact at higher scale and more ownership. Never knock Walmart.
- "What do you actually own at Walmart day to day?" → Audit-logging shared library (2M events/day, adopted by 12+ teams), the DC inventory search API, and the Spring Boot 3 migration.
- "Six services solo — really?" → Yes, at a 5-person startup: Beat, Coffee, Event-gRPC, Stir, SaaS Gateway, fake-follower Lambda. Design, build, deploy, on-call.
- "Why the startup → big-company → ?" → Each move was a real jump in scope. Startup taught me range; Walmart taught me scale and process.

**What NOT to say:**
- Don't list every project — three beats lands harder than ten. Pick depth on request.
- Don't bad-mouth PayU or Walmart, even mildly.
- Don't recite the stack like a keyword dump — name tech only where it carries weight (Kafka, ClickHouse, Spring Boot).

Want me to tune the ending for a specific company, or go deep on one of these projects?

transcript.text: Audit log project properly in proper format

blocks.md:
## "Walk me through the audit-logging project" — strong-tech HM / ownership + deliver-results lens
Story: W2 — `dv-api-common-libraries` shared audit library · Ownership / Invent and Simplify / Frugality · ~110s

### Situation
At Walmart Data Ventures, three product teams were each writing their own audit-logging code — basically copy-paste with small differences. And all of it ran through Splunk, which Walmart was decommissioning company-wide because the licensing was expensive. On top of that, suppliers like Pepsi kept asking "why did my API request fail?" and we had no clean way to show them.

### Task
My job was to kill the duplication and the Splunk dependency in one shot. Build one reusable audit-logging component any team could drop in, and route the data somewhere suppliers could actually query themselves.

### Action
I built it as a Spring Boot starter. The whole point was: a team adds one Maven dependency, and audit logging just turns on — no wiring, no config. That's the `spring.factories` auto-configuration doing the work.

> 💬 Layman version: "I packaged it so other teams get audit logging for free — they add one line to their build file and it works. They don't have to understand any of the internals."

The capture path had to be invisible performance-wise. A servlet filter intercepts each request, caches the body, then fires the audit record asynchronously — fire-and-forget, so the actual API response doesn't wait on it.

Under the hood: an idempotent Kafka producer, Avro serialization, a thread pool sized 6 core, 10 max, 100 queue. Then a three-tier pipeline — library publishes to Kafka, Kafka Connect sinks to GCS as Parquet with US/CA/MX routing, and suppliers query BigQuery external tables on top of that.

I shipped it design-to-prod in about five weeks.

### Result
Integration time for a new service went from two weeks to one day. We replaced Splunk audit logging — roughly $50K a month — with this pipeline at about $500 a month. That's basically a 99% cost cut. It runs around 2 million events a day at under 5ms P99, and suppliers now self-serve their own debugging.

---

**Technical depth — if they probe:**
- **Why a Spring Boot starter, not a shared util?** Auto-config via `spring.factories` means zero boilerplate for adopting teams — the dependency itself configures the beans. That's what drove the 2-weeks-to-1-day adoption.
- **Why fire-and-forget async?** Audit can't add latency to a customer's API call. The servlet filter caches the body with a `ContentCachingWrapper`, hands off to the thread pool, and returns immediately.
- **Why Kafka → GCS Parquet → BigQuery external tables?** Decouples capture from storage, and Parquet + BigQuery external tables means suppliers query their own data with plain SQL — no extra serving layer to run.
- **Idempotent producer + Avro:** idempotent so retries don't double-write records; Avro for a compact, schema'd payload across the pipeline.
- **The thread-pool sizing (6/10/100)** is the same config a senior later pushed back on — I have a whole story there if they want it (the DiscardPolicy fix).

**Likely follow-ups:**
- "What happened when the queue filled up?" → Honest answer: my first version could silently drop records. A senior caught it in review, I added a queue-depth gauge + 80% warning and switched to `CallerRunsPolicy`. Zero drops over 90 days after. (That's W3.)
- "Did this ever fail silently?" → Yes — separate incident, the Kafka Connect → GCS sink stopped writing for 5 days. Null SMT headers → NPE → silent retry → autoscaler → heap OOM. Led me to build real "is it processing correctly" alerting. (W1.)
- "How did suppliers actually query it?" → BigQuery external tables with row-level security — a policy tag on `supplier_id` so each supplier only sees their own rows. (W6.)
- "How did you get 3 teams to adopt it?" → The one-dependency design did most of the selling; one day of integration vs two weeks is an easy pitch.

**What NOT to say:**
- Don't claim it was flawless — the DiscardPolicy near-miss is actually a strength if they ask; lead with the win, but own that gap honestly.
- Don't overstate the cost number as profit — it's a licensing/infra cost reduction, ~$50K/mo → ~$500/mo.
- Don't drown them in the pipeline diagram up front; keep Kafka/Connect/BigQuery as the three beats and expand only if asked.

🎙️ **Say-it script:**

> So at Walmart, three product teams were each writing their own audit-logging code — basically copy-paste. And it all ran through Splunk, which the company was decommissioning because it was expensive. Plus suppliers kept asking "why did my request fail?" and we had no good way to show them.
>
> So I built it as a Spring Boot starter. The whole idea was — a team adds one Maven dependency and audit logging just turns on. No wiring. Behind that, a servlet filter grabs each request and fires the audit record asynchronously, so the API response never waits on it. Then it goes Kafka, into GCS as Parquet, and suppliers query it through BigQuery external tables with plain SQL.
>
> I shipped it design-to-prod in about five weeks. Integration for a new service went from two weeks to one day. And we replaced Splunk audit logging at roughly fifty grand a month with this pipeline at about five hundred — call it a 99% cut. It runs around 2 million events a day, under 5ms P99, and suppliers debug themselves now.

💬 **60-second version:**
> Three teams were duplicating audit-logging code and it all ran through Splunk, which was getting decommissioned. I built a Spring Boot starter — one Maven dependency and audit logging just turns on. It captures async via a servlet filter so there's no latency hit, then flows through Kafka to GCS Parquet to BigQuery, where suppliers query their own data. Integration dropped from two weeks to one day, and we cut audit cost from ~$50K a month to ~$500 — at 2 million events a day.

blocks.md:
Got it — you're asking what Avro serialization actually is, since at Good Creator Co. you sent payloads as JSON and Avro was new to you on the Walmart pipeline. Let me make this defensible for you. This is fundamentals, not a STAR answer.

## What "serialization" means first
When you put a message on Kafka, you can't send a Java object directly. You have to turn it into bytes. That conversion is **serialization**. On the other side, the consumer turns bytes back into an object — **deserialization**. JSON and Avro are just two different *formats* for those bytes.

> 💬 Plain English: "Serialization is just how I turn my object into bytes to put on Kafka, and back again on the other side. JSON is one way to do that. Avro is another."

## JSON vs Avro — the real difference
**JSON** sends the data *and* the field names, every single message, as text. So a message looks like `{"supplier_id": "PEP", "status": "FAILED"}`. The field names `supplier_id` and `status` get repeated in every message. It's human-readable, but bigger and slower.

**Avro** is binary, and the **schema lives separately** from the data. The schema says "field 1 is a string called supplier_id, field 2 is a string called status." The actual message on the wire is just the *values* as compact bytes — no field names repeated. The consumer uses the schema to know how to read those bytes.

> 💬 Layman line for the room: "JSON ships the field names with every message as text. Avro keeps the schema separate and only sends the values as compact binary — so it's much smaller and faster, especially at millions of messages a day."

## Why Avro matters on a Kafka pipeline (the 3 real reasons)
1. **Size and speed.** Binary + no repeated field names = smaller payloads. At ~2M events/day that's real network and storage savings. This is why it pairs naturally with Parquet/BigQuery downstream — both are columnar/binary.
2. **Schema enforcement.** A producer can't accidentally send a malformed message. The schema is the contract. With JSON, anyone can send anything and the consumer breaks at runtime.
3. **Schema evolution.** This is the big one. With a **Schema Registry**, you can add a new field later without breaking old consumers. Avro has rules for backward/forward compatibility — old consumers ignore new fields, new consumers fill defaults for missing ones.

> 💬 "The reason teams pick Avro over JSON on Kafka is schema evolution — I can add a field next quarter and not break every existing consumer. The Schema Registry holds the schema, so the message itself stays tiny."

## How to handle the honesty part in an interview
You don't have to pretend you've used Avro for years. Curiosity reads better than fake expertise. If they push and you're light on it, say:

> 💬 "At my previous company we were a small team doing about 100K events a day, so we just used JSON — it was simple and good enough for that scale. Avro was new to me at Walmart. Once I dug in, the win was schema evolution and the size cut at higher volume — that's why it's the right call for a pipeline serving multiple supplier teams."

That's actually a strong answer — it shows you right-size tools to the team (your G8 stack-defence instinct) *and* that you learn fast (your G11 story).

**One trap to avoid:** don't claim Avro is "always better than JSON." It isn't. JSON is fine for small scale, debugging, and human-readable APIs. Avro wins when you have high volume, multiple consumers, and a schema that will change over time. Saying that out loud shows judgment.

Want me to fold this into a clean "why did you choose Avro?" follow-up answer for the audit-log story, or go deeper on how the Schema Registry actually works?
